name: Hood County

actions.title: Proposed 6-Month Development Moratorium on Data Centers Voted Down
actions.summary: Hood County commissioners voted 3-2 to reject a proposed 6-month pause on new industrial development including data centers after a state senator warned counties lack legal authority to impose moratoriums and asked the attorney general to investigate.

contextBlurb: Hood County faces the potential buildout of four data centers, with residents warning that proposed sites sit in a recharge zone for the Upper Trinity Aquifer and within the Paluxy River watershed feeding Dinosaur Valley State Park. Opponents warned projects could consume up to 1 million gallons of water per day at full buildout and trigger construction of natural gas plants. The county's moratorium attempt was thwarted when State Sen. Paul Bettencourt wrote to Attorney General Ken Paxton arguing counties have no constitutional or statutory authority to impose development moratoriums.

name: Hays County

actions.title: County Judge Proposes Moratorium on High-Water-Use Development Permits
actions.summary: Hays County Judge Ruben Becerra announced plans to bring a moratorium on permits for large water-use developments — including data centers — to Commissioners Court, citing catastrophic drought conditions and strain on the Edwards Aquifer.

contextBlurb: Hays County Judge Ruben Becerra declared the regional water outlook 'a potential catastrophe,' citing an already drought-strained Edwards Aquifer, and proposed a moratorium on permits for any high-water-demand development including data centers. His proposal came the day before — and was galvanized by — the San Marcos City Council's 5-2 denial of a rezoning request for a 200-acre data center that could have used up to 70,000 gallons of water per day. Becerra framed the moratorium as a broader county-level safeguard ahead of additional data center proposals.

name: City of San Marcos

actions.title: City Council Denies Land-Use Map Change for CloudBurst / Highlander Data Center
actions.summary: San Marcos City Council voted 5-2 to deny a future land-use map change that would have cleared the way for a roughly 200-acre AI data center southwest of downtown, following an eight-hour public hearing overwhelmingly opposed by residents citing water scarcity and environmental risk.

actions.title: First Attempt: Supermajority Zoning Vote Failed
actions.summary: An earlier attempt in summer 2025 to pass a zoning change for the same data center site also failed because it could not achieve the required supermajority vote on the council.

contextBlurb: San Marcos became a flashpoint for Texas data center opposition when hundreds of residents showed up to a late-night council session to oppose a 200-acre AI data center near a recharge zone for the Edwards Aquifer. Grassroots organizers worked for over a year against the proposal, with water advocates warning the region is 'out of water.' The developer, Fort Worth-based Highlander SM One, may refile the request in six months under city rules.

name: City of Round Rock

actions.title: Skybox Data Center Rezoning (29.69 Acres) Approved Over Community Objection
actions.summary: Round Rock City Council unanimously approved a rezoning of ~29.69 acres from light industrial to a planned unit development for Skybox Datacenters despite more than 3,000 resident petition signatures opposing the project and hours of public comment citing noise, grid demand, air quality, and property values.

contextBlurb: Round Rock approved its ninth data center in February 2026, rezoning land adjacent to the Chandler Creek residential neighborhood for a Skybox facility despite a 3,000-signature petition and warnings from Public Citizen that low-frequency infrasound from large data centers would not be covered by the city's existing noise ordinance. Residents complained that eight prior data centers were approved before the community noticed, and that public-hearing signs were inadequately posted. Round Rock is part of a broader Williamson County cluster that includes facilities in Pflugerville, Hutto, and Georgetown.

name: Williamson County / City of Hutto

actions.title: Hutto Planning & Zoning Hears Rezoning Request for Zydeco 40-Acre Data Center Amid Resident Opposition
actions.summary: Hutto's Planning and Zoning Commission took up a request from Zydeco Development to rezone 40 acres and amend the city's future land-use map to allow a 'midscale' data center, replacing a planned 173-unit apartment complex; residents crowded City Hall to voice concerns.

contextBlurb: Williamson County's cities — Round Rock, Hutto, Pflugerville, and Georgetown — are experiencing a surge of data center proposals tied to Austin's growing tech market. Williamson County itself has limited authority over approvals, with zoning power resting with individual cities, creating a patchwork of policies. In April 2026, Hutto residents packed City Hall to oppose a Zydeco Development rezoning request for a midscale data center on land previously earmarked for housing.

name: Bosque County

actions.title: Community Opposition to Infrakey / CyrusOne Data Center in Ross; No Blocking Action Taken
actions.summary: Dozens of residents gathered at the Ross Volunteer Fire Department in December 2025 to oppose a large data center already under construction in Bosque County, but county officials declined to take blocking action, citing expected tax revenue increases of 120% over 30 years.

contextBlurb: A CyrusOne data center northwest of Waco in Bosque County was already under construction by late 2025, with county officials projecting a 120% boost to the county tax base worth $70 million over three decades. Despite organized community opposition meetings at the Ross Volunteer Fire Department, the county did not pursue any restrictive ordinance or pause. Texas Senate District 22 — which includes Bosque County — became a flashpoint, with Republican primary candidates making data center regulation a campaign issue.

name: City of Harlingen

actions.title: City-Owned Utility Executes Reclaimed Water Supply Agreement for RGV Data Center
actions.summary: Harlingen's city-owned utility entered an agreement to supply a data center developer with more than 4.6 million gallons per day of reclaimed wastewater, after residents addressed the City Commission opposing the project over water and electricity concerns but were told the site is outside city boundaries.

contextBlurb: Harlingen's municipal utility became one of the few Texas city utilities to directly negotiate a data-center water supply deal, committing over 4.6 million gallons per day of reclaimed wastewater to a developer behind the RGV Data Center Project, which projects approximately 1,000 new jobs. Residents raised concerns at public commission meetings but city officials said they lacked authority to intervene since the site is outside city limits. The use of reclaimed rather than potable water was offered as a mitigating factor by the developer.

name: City of College Station

actions.title: City Council Rejects Sale of City Property to Data Center Developer
actions.summary: College Station city leaders rejected the sale of city-owned property to a data center developer following mounting resident pressure over water and electricity concerns, and stipulated that any future data center deal would require the developer to secure its own power and water sources.

contextBlurb: College Station became one of the few Texas cities to use its property ownership as a lever to resist data center development, rejecting a land sale after residents pressured the council over power grid strain and water demand. Officials conditioned any future deal on the developer sourcing its own electricity and water, effectively raising the bar for development. The city is named alongside San Marcos, Amarillo, Waco, and Harlingen as an active site of grassroots data center opposition as of April 2026.
